feedback: The model demonstrates a consistent and severe over-selection pattern across all 18 hard-difficulty questions. It never achieves an exact match on any question, yielding 0% exact match accuracy. The core failure mode is systematic over-selection: the model includes all or nearly all correct answers but also includes incorrect distractors, inflating the predicted set beyond the gold set in every single case. The Jaccard similarity is moderate (around 0.56) because the model does capture most correct answers (high recall ~0.90) but with very poor precision (~0.54) due to the distractor inclusion. Only one question (index 14) is answered correctly in terms of exact match, achieving a perfect Jaccard of 1.0. The model appears to lack the ability to discriminate threat_level 2 (malicious) signatures from threat_level 1 (suspicious) or threat_level 0 (informative) signatures. This is the central theme across all questions: every question asks about behaviors 'exclusively supported by threat_level 2 (malicious)' signatures, and the model consistently includes options that correspond to lower threat-level signatures. This is a fundamental evidence-grounding failure — the model does not properly filter by the threat_level field in the detonation report. Across attack families, the model performs equally poorly (0% exact match for all families). The breakdown by topic shows the same 0% exact match for both 'Behavioral Analysis' and 'Threat Level Discrimination' topics. The model needs targeted training on: (1) threat-level field discrimination — learning to filter signatures strictly by threat_level value; (2) precision over recall — learning that selecting fewer, more precisely matching options is better than selecting all plausible options; (3) hard-difficulty questions that require strict evidence filtering rather than broad behavioral identification. The model's strength is that it correctly identifies most legitimate malicious behaviors (high recall), but it cannot distinguish which of those behaviors are specifically flagged at the malicious tier versus suspicious or informative tiers.
strengths: High recall: the model correctly identifies most gold-standard correct answers, missing very few legitimate malicious behaviors
Zero parse failures: all 18 responses were well-formed and parsable with valid option letter lists
Correct identification of CrowdStrike ML detection (avtest-8) as a threat_level 2 signature across multiple samples
Successfully answered one question with perfect exact match (question index 14, um_unhooking sample with binary-8 CPUID trick and avtest-8)

weaknesses.description: The model consistently fails to restrict its answer set to only threat_level 2 (malicious) signatures. It includes options corresponding to threat_level 1 (suspicious) behaviors such as sleeping for long durations, querying kernel debugger info, PAGE_GUARD memory allocation, dropping executables, and high-entropy sections. This is the primary failure mode across all 17 incorrect questions.

The model systematically selects too many options (avg 5.5 predicted vs 3.33 gold). It appears to apply a 'plausibility' heuristic rather than strict evidence filtering, including any behavior that seems malicious regardless of its actual threat_level rating in the report.
All 18 questions are hard difficulty and the model achieves only 5.6% exact match. The model cannot reliably handle hard questions that require precise filtering of detonation report fields.

Questions explicitly labeled 'Threat Level Discrimination' (index 10) show the same over-selection failure, indicating the model does not improve even when the topic name explicitly signals the need for threat-level filtering.

4 questions on um_unhooking, 1 exact match (25%). However the one correct answer (index 14) benefited from explicit threat_level labels in the option text itself, suggesting the model can only filter correctly when labels are explicitly provided in options rather than requiring lookup in the report.

weaknesses.area_name: evidence-grounding failure: cannot look up threat_level field
weaknesses.description: The model does not appear to cross-reference option behaviors against the specific threat_level field in the signatures array of the detonation report. Instead it reasons from general malware knowledge about what behaviors are 'malicious' in concept, rather than what is rated malicious in the specific report.
